sanskrit_devanagari:
अधिभूतं क्षरो भावः पुरुषश्चाधिदैवतम् ।
अधियज्ञोऽहमेवात्र देहे देहभृतां वर ॥ ४ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
adhibhūtaṁ kṣaro bhāvaḥ puruṣaś cādhidaivatam |
adhiyajño 'ham evātra dehe deha-bhṛtāṁ vara || 4 ||

translations.verbatim_quote: O best of the embodied beings, the physical nature, which is constantly changing, is called adhibhūta [the material manifestation]. The universal form of the Lord, which includes all the demigods, like those of the sun and moon, is called adhidaiva. And I, the Supreme Lord, represented as the Supersoul in the heart of every embodied being, am called adhiyajña [the Lord of sacrifice].

O best of the embodied souls, the ever-changing physical manifestation is called adhibhuta, the universal form of God, which presides over the celestial gods in this creation, is called adhidaiva. I, who dwell in the heart of every living being, am called adhiyajña, or the Lord of all sacrifices.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The physical nature is constantly changing. Material bodies generally pass through six stages: they are born, they grow, they remain for some time, they produce some by-products, they dwindle, and then they vanish. This physical nature is called adhibhūta.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 8.4 Adhibhutam, that which exists in the physical plane, consists of the ksarah bhavah, mutable entity, anything whatsoever that has origination. Purushah is Hiranyagarbha, who resides in the Sun and sustains the organs of all creatures. He is adhi-daivatam, the entity existing in the divine plane. Adhiyajnah is the Deity, Vishnu, presiding over all sacrifices — Aham eva, I Myself, who am that very Vishnu; in this body.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads adhibhūta as the perishable existence (kṣaraḥ bhāvaḥ); puruṣa as Hiranyagarbha residing in the Sun; adhidaivata as the divine-plane entity; adhiyajña as Vishnu presiding over all sacrifices, identical with Krishna himself dwelling in the body. The 'Sacrifice is indeed Vishnu' Vedic text (Tai. Sam. 1.7.4) is invoked. Three of the seven questions are answered with maximum doctrinal precision.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 8.4 The perishable existences which have been declared as fit to be known by the seekers of wealth, power etc., form the Adhibhuta. Adhidaivata connotes Purusa, superior to divinities like Indra, Prajapati and others, the experiencer of sound etc. I alone am connoted by the term Adhiyajna. Indra and others, to whom sacrifices are made, form My body. I dwell as their Self and I alone am the object of worship by sacrifice.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads adhibhūta as the perishable evolutes of material elements (sound, touch, form, taste, smell) sought by seekers of prosperity. adhidaivata is puruṣa, superior to Indra and Prajapati, the experiencer of sensory enjoyments. adhiyajña is Krishna himself, with Indra and the other sacrifice-recipients forming his body — Krishna dwells as their Self and is the sole true object of worship. The Vishishtadvaita reading preserves the inner-Lord-of-the-offering frame.

positions.rendering: the universal form of the Lord, including all the demigods like the sun and moon
the universal form of God, presiding over the celestial gods
Hiranyagarbha, who resides in the Sun and sustains the organs of all creatures (Advaita reading via cosmic Person)
Purusa, superior to Indra and Prajapati, the experiencer of sensory enjoyments (Vishishtadvaita)

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: puruṣa here is named as the adhidaivata. Shankara reads this as Hiranyagarbha-in-the-Sun (the Vedic cosmic Person who sustains organs of all creatures). Ramanuja reads puruṣa as superior to all demigods including Indra and Prajapati, the experiencer of sensory enjoyments. Both traditions read this as the cosmic-divine principle, but the metaphysics differ — Advaita places Hiranyagarbha within the nondual Brahman; Vishishtadvaita reads puruṣa as related-but-superior. Modern translations conflate to 'universal form' or 'celestial gods'. This is a doctrinally specific term resistant to engineering translation.

positions.rendering: I, the Supreme Lord, represented as the Supersoul in the heart of every embodied being, am called adhiyajña
I, who dwell in the heart of every living being, am called adhiyajña
Vishnu presiding over all sacrifices — I Myself, who am that very Vishnu, in this body
I alone am connoted by adhiyajña; Indra and others form My body; I dwell as their Self

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: This is the chapter's structural pivot — Krishna identifies himself directly with the adhiyajña, dwelling in this very body. All three commentarial traditions agree on the surface (Krishna IS the adhiyajña in the body) and diverge on the metaphysics: Advaita identifies Krishna with Vishnu of the Vedic-sacrificial-formula; Vishishtadvaita reads Krishna as the Self of all sacrifice-recipients. The Vedic text 'yajño vai viṣṇuḥ' (Tai. Sam. 1.7.4) is invoked by Shankara. The verse closes 8.1's first six questions; 8.5 will answer the seventh.

literal_meaning: adhibhūta is the perishable existence. The puruṣa is the adhidaiva. And I, here in the body, am the adhiyajña, O best of the embodied.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna answers questions four through six of Arjuna's seven. (4) adhibhūta = kṣaraḥ bhāvaḥ, the perishable existence — the constantly-changing physical manifestation. (5) adhidaiva = puruṣa, the cosmic Person; Shankara identifies this with Hiranyagarbha-in-the-Sun, Ramanuja with the puruṣa superior to Indra and Prajapati. (6) adhiyajña = Krishna himself in this body. The verse's structural pivot is the explicit self-identification: 'I, here in the body, am the adhiyajña.' The Lord-of-the-offering is not external; he resides within. The traditions diverge on the metaphysics of puruṣa as adhidaivata but agree precisely on Krishna's self-identification with adhiyajña. The seventh question (manner of knowing-at-time-of-death) is held over to 8.5.
